attrs.alt: Role assignment = Security Principal + Role Definition + Scope

content.text: What Is a Role Assignment

A role assignment is the mechanism that grants access in Azure RBAC. It binds three elements: a security principal (who gets access), a role definition (what they can do), and a scope (where the access applies). The assignment itself is stored as an Azure resource of type Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments and can be managed through the portal, PowerShell, Azure CLI, REST API, or IaC.

Role assignments are additive — a principal's effective permissions at any scope are the union of all applicable role assignments. There is no concept of 'replacing' an assignment with a narrower one; you can only add or remove assignments. Removing an assignment takes effect within a few minutes as the authorization cache refreshes.
Creating Role Assignments

# Create a role assignment using PowerShell

# Assign Contributor to a user at a resource group scope
$user = Get-MgUser -Filter "UserPrincipalName eq 'alice@contoso.com'"
$rg = Get-AzResourceGroup -Name 'prod-app-rg'
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId $user.Id `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' `
  -Scope $rg.ResourceId

# Assign to a group
$group = Get-MgGroup -Filter "DisplayName eq 'DevOps Engineers'"
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId $group.Id `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Reader' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>'

# Assign to a managed identity — specify principalType to avoid slow graph lookup
$mi = Get-AzUserAssignedIdentity -ResourceGroupName 'identity-rg' -Name 'app-worker-mi'
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId $mi.PrincipalId `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Key Vault Secrets User' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/prod-rg/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/prod-kv' `
  -PrincipalType ServicePrincipal   # avoids Entra ID graph call delay

# Using Azure CLI
az role assignment create `
  --assignee alice@contoso.com `
  --role 'Contributor' `
  --resource-group prod-app-rg

# Using role definition ID instead of name (faster, avoids name lookup)
# Contributor = b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId $user.Id `
  -RoleDefinitionId 'b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c' `
  -Scope $rg.ResourceId

Always specify -PrincipalType (User, Group, ServicePrincipal) when assigning to a managed identity or service principal. Without it, Azure makes a graph call that can add seconds of latency or fail if you lack Graph permissions.
The subscription limit is 4,000 role assignments. Management groups allow 2,000. Hitting these limits causes 'RoleAssignmentLimitExceeded' errors — prefer group-based assignments to minimize count.
Role assignments take effect within 1-5 minutes due to ARM authorization cache TTL. New tokens issued after assignment take effect immediately; existing cached tokens may take up to 5 minutes.
You need Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments/write permission (granted by Owner or User Access Administrator) to create assignments.
Listing and Auditing Role Assignments

# List all role assignments in the current subscription
Get-AzRoleAssignment | Select-Object DisplayName, RoleDefinitionName, ObjectType, Scope |
    Format-Table -AutoSize

# Filter by principal
Get-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId '<principal-id>'

# Filter by scope (only assignments at that exact scope, not inherited)
Get-AzRoleAssignment -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/prod-app-rg' |
    Where-Object { $_.Scope -eq '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/prod-app-rg' }

# Filter by role name
Get-AzRoleAssignment -RoleDefinitionName 'Owner'

# Include classic administrators (Account Administrator, Service Administrator)
Get-AzRoleAssignment -IncludeClassicAdministrators

# Azure CLI: list with output table for quick review
az role assignment list --all --include-inherited `
    --query '[].{Who:principalName,Type:principalType,Role:roleDefinitionName,Where:scope}' `
    --output table

# Find orphaned role assignments (principal deleted — ObjectType = Unknown)
Get-AzRoleAssignment | Where-Object { $_.ObjectType -eq 'Unknown' } |
    Select-Object RoleDefinitionName, ObjectId, Scope
Flag / Filter
What It Returns
-ObjectId <guid>
All assignments for one principal, across all scopes
-Scope <path>
Assignments at that scope AND all parent scopes (inherited)
-RoleDefinitionName <name>
All principals who hold that role in the subscription
-ResourceGroupName <name>
Assignments within that resource group (direct + inherited)
-IncludeClassicAdministrators
Also returns legacy Account/Service Administrator entries
ObjectType = 'Unknown'
Orphaned assignments — principal no longer exists in Entra ID
Removing Role Assignments

Removing a role assignment revokes the permissions granted by it immediately (within the cache TTL). If the same principal has other assignments that grant overlapping permissions, those remain. You must specify all three components (principal, role, scope) to uniquely identify an assignment for removal.

# Remove a specific role assignment
Remove-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId '<principal-id>' `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/prod-app-rg'

# Remove using piped Get result
Get-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId '<principal-id>' -RoleDefinitionName 'Reader' |
    Remove-AzRoleAssignment

# Bulk remove all assignments for a deleted user (orphaned)
$orphanId = '<deleted-user-object-id>'
Get-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $orphanId | Remove-AzRoleAssignment

# Remove all assignments of a specific role across the subscription
Get-AzRoleAssignment -RoleDefinitionName 'Virtual Machine Contributor' |
    Remove-AzRoleAssignment

# Azure CLI remove
az role assignment delete `
  --assignee alice@contoso.com `
  --role 'Contributor' `
  --resource-group prod-app-rg

Remove orphaned role assignments (ObjectType = Unknown) regularly. They waste assignment quota and obscure the true access picture. A principal that was deleted from Entra ID no longer exists and cannot use the access, but the assignment slot is still consumed.
Time-Bound Assignments with PIM

Entra ID Privileged Identity Management (PIM) extends role assignments with time constraints. An assignment can be set to expire after a fixed duration (e.g., 30 days) rather than being permanent. PIM also supports eligible assignments, where the principal must explicitly activate the role (with MFA, justification, and optional approval) before the permissions are active.

# PIM (P2): create a time-bound active assignment (expires after N days)
$userId = (Get-MgUser -Filter "UserPrincipalName eq 'bob@contoso.com'").Id

# Create time-bound ACTIVE assignment (active immediately, expires in 30 days)
$params = @{
    action = 'adminAssign'
    justification = 'Temporary access for project Q3 deployment'
    roleDefinitionId = 'b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c'  # Contributor
    directoryScopeId = '/subscriptions/<sub-id>'
    principalId = $userId
    scheduleInfo = @{
        startDateTime = (Get-Date -Format 'o')
        expiration = @{
            type = 'AfterDateTime'
            endDateTime = (Get-Date).AddDays(30).ToString('o')
        }
    }
}
New-MgRoleManagementDirectoryRoleAssignmentScheduleRequest -BodyParameter $params

# List all PIM active assignments at resource scope
Get-AzRoleAssignmentScheduleInstance -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>' |
    Where-Object { $_.AssignmentType -eq 'Activated' } |
    Select-Object PrincipalName, RoleDefinitionName, StartDateTime, EndDateTime

# List ELIGIBLE assignments (not yet activated)
Get-AzRoleEligibilityScheduleInstance -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>' |
    Select-Object PrincipalName, RoleDefinitionName, StartDateTime, EndDateTime
Assignment Type
When to Use
Permanent Active
Service accounts, automation identities, break-glass accounts
Time-bound Active
Temporary project access, contractors, incident response
Eligible (PIM)
Privileged roles that humans hold but rarely need (Owner, Contributor, etc.)
Role Assignments in Infrastructure as Code

Defining role assignments in IaC alongside the resources they protect gives you version history, peer review, and drift detection. The ARM resource name must be a GUID — use the guid() function with stable seeds to make deployments idempotent. Changing the seed generates a new GUID, creating a duplicate assignment.
Conditional Role Assignments (ABAC)

Azure Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) lets you add conditions to role assignments on Storage and Key Vault data plane roles. A condition is a CEL expression evaluated at request time — if it evaluates to false, the operation is denied even though the role grants it. Conditions enable fine-grained access without creating custom roles: for example, allow blob read only if the container name matches a prefix, or allow blob write only if specific tags are present.
Conditions work on Storage Blob Data Reader/Contributor/Owner and Key Vault data plane roles in preview.
The condition language uses @Resource[] and @Request[] selectors to access resource attributes and request parameters.
ConditionVersion must be '2.0' — the only currently supported version.
Conditions are attached per assignment — the same role can be assigned unconditionally to one principal and conditionally to another at the same scope.

# Conditional role assignments (ABAC) are supported on Storage and Key Vault data plane roles.
# A condition restricts an assignment further — e.g., allow blob read only on containers named 'public-*'

# Assign Storage Blob Data Reader with a condition on container name
$condition = "@Resource[Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices/containers:name]"
$condition += " StringLike 'public-*'"

New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId '<group-id>' `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Storage Blob Data Reader' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/data-rg/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/proddata01' `
  -Condition $condition `
  -ConditionVersion '2.0'

# Condition: allow creating blobs only with specific tags
$tagCondition = (
    "@Request[Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices/containers/blobs:tags&\$keys@OData.Community.Display.V1.Tag]"
    + " ForAllOfAnyValues:StringEquals {'Environment','DataClass'}"
)
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId '<writer-group-id>' `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Storage Blob Data Contributor' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/data-rg/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/proddata01' `
  -Condition $tagCondition `
  -ConditionVersion '2.0'

# List assignments with conditions
Get-AzRoleAssignment | Where-Object { $_.Condition } |
    Select-Object DisplayName, RoleDefinitionName, Condition

ABAC conditions are an alternative to creating dozens of narrowly scoped role assignments. If you find yourself assigning Reader to 10 principals but each needs access to a different storage container, one conditional assignment per principal at the account scope is cleaner than 10 separate resource-level assignments.
Troubleshooting Access Problems

When a user reports they cannot perform an operation, work through the access model systematically. The most common causes are: the assignment exists but on a group the user joined after their last sign-in (requires re-login to get a new token), the assignment grants Actions but the operation requires DataActions, or an Azure Policy deny effect is blocking the request independently of RBAC.

# Troubleshoot: user reports they can't access a resource

# Step 1: confirm their identity
$userId = (Get-MgUser -Filter "UserPrincipalName eq 'alice@contoso.com'").Id

# Step 2: list all role assignments that reach the resource (including inherited)
Get-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $userId -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/prod-rg' |
    Select-Object RoleDefinitionName, Scope, ObjectType

# Step 3: check group memberships (assignments to groups the user belongs to)
Get-MgUserMemberOf -UserId $userId | Select-Object @{N='Name';E={$_.AdditionalProperties['displayName']}}

# Step 4: check for deny assignments
Get-AzDenyAssignment -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/prod-rg'

# Step 5: verify the role's actual operations
Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Contributor' | Select-Object -ExpandProperty Actions

# Step 6: check Azure Policy deny effects that may be blocking the request
Get-AzPolicyState -ResourceGroupName 'prod-rg' | Where-Object { $_.ComplianceState -eq 'NonCompliant' }

# Common causes:
# - Assignment exists but cache hasn't refreshed (wait 5 min, re-issue token)
# - Assignment is on a group the user joined after their last login (re-login)
# - DataActions required but only Actions are granted (Storage blob vs account)
# - Azure Policy deny effect blocking the specific operation
# - Assignment is at wrong scope (sibling RG, not the target RG)
Symptom
Likely Cause
Fix
Access denied immediately after assignment
ARM authorization cache (5 min TTL)
Wait 5 minutes; force a new token with az account get-access-token
Assignment exists but no access
Assignment is on a group; user joined after last login
User logs out and back in to refresh group membership in token
Can list resource but can't read data
Role grants Actions but not DataActions
Assign a role with appropriate DataActions (e.g., Storage Blob Data Reader)
Access denied despite Contributor role
Azure Policy deny effect
Check Get-AzPolicyState for NonCompliant policies with deny effect
Get-AzRoleAssignment shows nothing
Assignment is at a parent scope (subscription/MG)
Add -Scope param or check parent scope separately
Managing the Assignment Quota

Each Azure subscription has a limit of 4,000 role assignments. Each management group allows 2,000. These limits are per-scope — assignments at the subscription level count toward the subscription limit, not the management group limit. Large organizations with many fine-grained assignments can approach these limits. The solution is always group-based consolidation: replace N individual user assignments with 1 group assignment.
Summary
Role assignments are the binding that connects a principal, a permission set, and a scope. Key operational points:
Create assignments with -PrincipalType specified to avoid graph lookup latency, especially for service principals and managed identities.
Role assignments are additive — removing one reduces permissions but doesn't affect access granted by other assignments.
4,000 assignments per subscription is the hard limit — use group-based assignments to stay well below it.
Audit regularly for orphaned assignments (ObjectType = Unknown) and remove them to reclaim quota and maintain a clean access model.
Use PIM for any role a human holds at subscription scope or above — time-bound and eligible assignments reduce standing privileged access.
Manage assignments in IaC with deterministic guid() names to keep your RBAC model reproducible and auditable.
